[Vince Cable](/wiki/Vince_Cable "Vince Cable") Simon Hughes [Malcolm Bruce](/wiki/Malcolm_Bruce "Malcolm Bruce")

Nicholas William Peter Clegg  (1967-01-07) 7 January 1967 (age 59)  [Chalfont St Giles](/wiki/Chalfont_St_Giles "Chalfont St Giles"), [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire"), England

* [Robinson College, Cambridge](/wiki/Robinson_College,_Cambridge "Robinson College, Cambridge") ([BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts")) * [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") * [College of Europe](/wiki/College_of_Europe "College of Europe") ([MA](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts"))
[Official website](http://www.nickclegg.org.uk/) [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q189947#P856 "Edit this at Wikidata")

(born 7 January 1967) is a British retired politician and media executive who served as [Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") from 2010 to 2015 and as [Leader of the Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Liberal_Democrats "Leader of the Liberal Democrats") from 2007 to 2015. He was [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) for [Sheffield Hallam](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Hallam (UK Parliament constituency)") from 2005 to 2017. An "[Orange Book](/wiki/The_Orange_Book "The Orange Book")" liberal,[[2]](#cite_note-orangebook-5) he has been associated with both [socially liberal](/wiki/Socially_liberal "Socially liberal") and [economically liberal](/wiki/Economically_liberal "Economically liberal") policies.[[3]](#cite_note-6)[[4]](#cite_note-auto2-7)

**Sir Nicholas William Peter Clegg** (born 7 January 1967) is a British retired politician and media executive who served as [Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") from 2010 to 2015 and as [Leader of the Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Liberal_Democrats "Leader of the Liberal Democrats") from 2007 to 2015. He was [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) for [Sheffield Hallam](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Hallam (UK Parliament constituency)") from 2005 to 2017. An "[Orange Book](/wiki/The_Orange_Book "The Orange Book")" liberal,[[2]](#cite_note-orangebook-5) he has been associated with both [socially liberal](/wiki/Socially_liberal "Socially liberal") and [economically liberal](/wiki/Economically_liberal "Economically liberal") policies.[[3]](#cite_note-6)[[4]](#cite_note-auto2-7)

Born in [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire"), Clegg was educated at [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School") before going on to study at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") and [College of Europe](/wiki/College_of_Europe "College of Europe"). He worked as a journalist for the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* before becoming a [Member of the European Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") (MEP) in 1999.[[5]](#cite_note-8) After his election to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in 2005, Clegg served in a variety of leadership roles in the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), most notably as [spokesperson for Home Affairs](/wiki/Liberal_Democrat_Home_Affairs_spokesperson "Liberal Democrat Home Affairs spokesperson"), before being elected to succeed [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell") as party leader in [2007](/wiki/2007_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2007 Liberal Democrats leadership election"). During his tenure as leader, Clegg said that the Liberal Democrats had [transcended left and right-wing politics](/wiki/Triangulation_(politics) "Triangulation (politics)") and described the party as [radical centrist](/wiki/Radical_centrism "Radical centrism"). He supported reduced taxes, electoral reform, cuts on defence spending and an increased focus on environmental issues.

In the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), Clegg's Liberal Democrats won 57 seats in the House of Commons. The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), which failed to receive a majority, formed [a coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") with the Liberal Democrats, and Clegg was appointed by Conservative leader [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") to serve as his Deputy Prime Minister. In [this capacity](/wiki/Nick_Clegg%27s_tenure_as_Deputy_Prime_Minister "Nick Clegg's tenure as Deputy Prime Minister"), he became the first leader of the Liberal Democrats to answer for the [Prime Minister's Questions](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Questions "Prime Minister's Questions"), and used his influence in the position to pass the [Fixed-term Parliaments Act](/wiki/Fixed-term_Parliaments_Act_2011 "Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011").[[6]](#cite_note-takebackpower1-9) Controversy arose during this time surrounding the Liberal Democrats' decision to abandon their pledge to oppose increases in tuition fees, which had previously been a key issue that won the party support from students.[[7]](#cite_note-libdems6-10) During the party's time in coalition, the Liberal Democrats saw a significant drop in support,[[8]](#cite_note-thisislondon1-11) and the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election") left the party with just 8 seats, which resulted in Clegg's ousting as Deputy Prime Minister and his resignation as party leader.[[9]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-13)

In 2016, following a [referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") in which a majority supported leaving the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), Clegg returned to the [Liberal Democrat frontbench](/wiki/Liberal_Democrat_frontbench_team "Liberal Democrat frontbench team"), concurrently serving as Spokesperson for Exiting the European Union and for International Trade from July 2016 to June 2017. In the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"), Clegg was defeated in his constituency of Sheffield Hallam by [Jared O'Mara](/wiki/Jared_O%27Mara "Jared O'Mara") of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)").[[11]](#cite_note-telegraph2-14) He was appointed a [Knight Bachelor](/wiki/Knight_Bachelor "Knight Bachelor") in the [2018 New Year Honours](/wiki/2018_New_Year_Honours "2018 New Year Honours") for political and public service.

Clegg left politics after losing his seat and relocated to the United States, where in October 2018 he became vice‑president of global affairs and communications at Facebook, Inc. (renamed [Meta Platforms](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms") in 2021). He was promoted to president for global affairs in 2022. Clegg announced he would step down from the role and leave Meta in 2025.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-15)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-16)

In March 2026 Clegg has joined the board of British AI Start up Nscale.[[14]](#cite_note-17)
