Nicholas William Peter Clegg was born in [Chalfont St Giles](/wiki/Chalfont_St_Giles "Chalfont St Giles"), Buckinghamshire, on 7 January 1967.[[15]](#cite_note-18) He was the third of four children born of Hermance van den Wall Bake and [Nicholas Peter Clegg](/wiki/Nicholas_P._Clegg "Nicholas P. Clegg"), chairman of United Trust Bank[[16]](#cite_note-19) and a former trustee of the [Daiwa Anglo-Japanese Foundation](/wiki/Daiwa_Anglo-Japanese_Foundation "Daiwa Anglo-Japanese Foundation")[[17]](#cite_note-20) (where [Ken Clarke](/wiki/Ken_Clarke "Ken Clarke") was an adviser).[[18]](#cite_note-21)

Clegg has one-quarter [Baltic-German](/wiki/Baltic-German "Baltic-German") ancestry. His paternal grandmother, [Baroness Kira von Engelhardt](/wiki/Engelhardt_family "Engelhardt family") née Zakrevskaja (1909–2005), of [Smolensk](/wiki/Smolensk "Smolensk"), was a Baltic-German noblewoman, niece of [Moura Budberg](/wiki/Moura_Budberg "Moura Budberg"), adventuress and suspected [double agent](/wiki/Double_agent "Double agent"),[[19]](#cite_note-22) and the granddaughter of [attorney general](/wiki/Attorney_general "Attorney general") of the [Imperial Russian](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") Senate, Ignatiy Platonovich Zakrevsky.[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24) Through this Russian connection, Clegg is distantly related to [Michael Ignatieff](/wiki/Michael_Ignatieff "Michael Ignatieff"), leader of the [Liberal Party of Canada](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") from 2008 to 2011.[[22]](#cite_note-25)

His English grandfather was [Hugh Anthony Clegg](/wiki/Hugh_Clegg_(physician) "Hugh Clegg (physician)"), editor of the *[British Medical Journal](/wiki/The_BMJ "The BMJ")* for 35 years.[[23]](#cite_note-telegraph1-26)

Clegg's mother is Dutch[[24]](#cite_note-27) and was [interned](/wiki/Interned "Interned"), along with her family, by the [Japanese military](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") in [Batavia](/wiki/History_of_Jakarta "History of Jakarta") (Jakarta) in the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") (Indonesia) during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). She met Clegg's father during a visit to England in 1956,[[23]](#cite_note-telegraph1-26) and they married on 1 August 1959.

Clegg is [multilingual](/wiki/Multilingual "Multilingual"). He speaks English, French, Dutch, German, and Spanish.[[25]](#cite_note-lang1-28)[[26]](#cite_note-lang2-29)[[27]](#cite_note-lang3-30) His background has informed his politics. He says, "There is simply not a shred of racism in me, as a person whose whole family is formed by flight from persecution, from different people in different generations. It's what I am. It's one of the reasons I am a liberal."[[28]](#cite_note-31) His Dutch mother instilled in him "a degree of scepticism about the entrenched [class configurations in British society](/wiki/Social_structure_of_the_United_Kingdom "Social structure of the United Kingdom")".[[29]](#cite_note-ekklesia-32)

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Westminster_School_Arch.jpg/250px-Westminster_School_Arch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westminster_School_Arch.jpg)

[Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School")

Clegg was educated at two [private schools](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom"): at [Caldicott School](/wiki/Caldicott_School "Caldicott School") in [Farnham Royal](/wiki/Farnham_Royal "Farnham Royal") in South [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire"), where he was joint Head Prefect in 1980,[[30]](#cite_note-onlinenigeria-33)[[31]](#cite_note-34) and later at [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School") in [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London"). As a 16-year-old [exchange student](/wiki/Exchange_student "Exchange student") in Munich, he and a friend drunkenly set fire to what he called "the leading collection of [cacti](/wiki/Cacti "Cacti") in Germany".[[32]](#cite_note-35) When news of the incident was reported during his time as [Liberal Democrat Home Affairs spokesperson](/wiki/Liberal_Democrat_Home_Affairs_spokesperson "Liberal Democrat Home Affairs spokesperson"), Clegg said he was "not proud" of it.[[33]](#cite_note-36) He was arrested and not formally charged, but performed a type of [community service](/wiki/Community_service "Community service").[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[4]](#cite_note-auto2-7)

Clegg spent a [gap year](/wiki/Gap_year "Gap year") working as a skiing instructor in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), before going on to [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") in 1986, where he studied Archaeology and Anthropology at [Robinson College](/wiki/Robinson_College "Robinson College").[[35]](#cite_note-bb-38) He was active in the student theatre, acting in a production of *[The Normal Heart](/wiki/The_Normal_Heart "The Normal Heart")* under the director [Sam Mendes](/wiki/Sam_Mendes "Sam Mendes").[[35]](#cite_note-bb-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39)[[37]](#cite_note-typepad1-40) He was also captain of his college's tennis team, and campaigned for the human rights organisation [Survival International](/wiki/Survival_International "Survival International").[[38]](#cite_note-41) Clegg spent the summer of 1989 as an office junior in [Postipankki](/wiki/Postipankki "Postipankki") bank in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki").[[39]](#cite_note-42)

It has been alleged that Clegg joined the [Cambridge University Conservative Association](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Conservative_Association "Cambridge University Conservative Association") between 1986 and 1987. Clegg has maintained he has "no recollection of that whatsoever". However, Conservative MP [Greg Hands](/wiki/Greg_Hands "Greg Hands") has a record of CUCA members for 1986–1987, and Clegg's name appears on the list. Hands noted that "for the avoidance of any doubt, there was only one 'N Clegg' at Robinson College ... [he] is listed in the 'Robinson College Record', under 'Freshmen 1986'.[[40]](#cite_note-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45)[[43]](#cite_note-blogs-46) He graduated with an upper second class honours (2:1) degree in social anthropology.[[44]](#cite_note-lrb-47)

After university, he was awarded a scholarship to study for a year at the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"), where he wrote a thesis on the political philosophy of the [Deep Green movement](/wiki/Deep_ecology "Deep ecology"). He then moved to New York City, where he worked as an intern under [Christopher Hitchens](/wiki/Christopher_Hitchens "Christopher Hitchens") at *[The Nation](/wiki/The_Nation "The Nation")*, a [progressive liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") magazine, where he fact-checked Hitchens's articles.[[45]](#cite_note-BBC_Daily_Politics_profile-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49)

Clegg next moved to [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), where he worked alongside [Guy Spier](/wiki/Guy_Spier "Guy Spier") for six months as a trainee in the G24 coordination unit which delivered aid to the countries of the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). After the internship he studied for a master's degree at the [College of Europe](/wiki/College_of_Europe "College of Europe") in [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), a university for European studies in Belgium, where he met his wife, [Miriam González Durántez](/wiki/Miriam_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Dur%C3%A1ntez "Miriam González Durántez"), a lawyer and the daughter of a Spanish senator.[[37]](#cite_note-typepad1-40) Nick Clegg is an alumnus of the "[Mozart Promotion](/wiki/College_of_Europe#Promotions "College of Europe")" (1991–92) of the College of Europe.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

## Careers before politics

Between 1992 and 1993, he was employed by GJW Government Relations Ltd, which lobbied on behalf of [Libya](/wiki/Libyan_Arab_Jamahiriya "Libyan Arab Jamahiriya").[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52)

In 1993, Clegg won the inaugural *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*' David Thomas Prize, in remembrance of an *FT* journalist killed on assignment in [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") in 1991. He was later sent to Hungary, where he wrote articles about the mass privatisation of industries in the former [communist bloc](/wiki/Communist_bloc "Communist bloc").[[37]](#cite_note-typepad1-40)

He took up a post at the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") in April 1994, working in the [TACIS](/wiki/TACIS "TACIS") aid programme to the [former Soviet Union](/wiki/Former_Soviet_Union "Former Soviet Union"). For two years, Clegg was responsible for developing direct aid programmes in Central Asia and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") worth €50 million. He was involved in negotiations with Russia on airline overflight rights, and launched a conference in [Tashkent](/wiki/Tashkent "Tashkent") in 1993 that founded [TRACECA](/wiki/TRACECA "TRACECA")—an international transport programme for the development of a [transport corridor](/wiki/Transport_corridor "Transport corridor") for Europe, the Caucasus and Asia. [Vice-President](/wiki/Vice-President_of_the_European_Commission "Vice-President of the European Commission") and [Trade Commissioner](/wiki/European_Commissioner_for_Trade "European Commissioner for Trade") [Leon Brittan](/wiki/Leon_Brittan "Leon Brittan") then offered him a job in his private office, as a European Union policy adviser and speechwriter. As part of this role, Clegg was in charge of the EC negotiating team on Chinese and Russian accession talks to the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization").[[37]](#cite_note-typepad1-40)

### Written publications

Clegg has written extensively, publishing and contributing to a large number of pamphlets and books. With [Richard Grayson](/wiki/Richard_Grayson_(academic) "Richard Grayson (academic)") he wrote a book in 2002 about the importance of devolution in secondary education systems, based on comparative research across Europe. The final conclusions included the idea of [pupil premiums](/wiki/Pupil_premium "Pupil premium") so that children from poorer backgrounds receive the additional resources their educational needs require.

He wrote a controversial pamphlet for the Centre for European Reform advocating devolution and evolution of the European Union, and contributed to the 2004 *[Orange Book](/wiki/The_Orange_Book:_Reclaiming_Liberalism "The Orange Book: Reclaiming Liberalism")*, where he offered [market liberal](/wiki/Classical_liberalism "Classical liberalism") solutions for reform of European institutions.[[2]](#cite_note-orangebook-5) He co-authored a pamphlet with Duncan Brack arguing for a wholesale reform of world trade rules to allow room for a greater emphasis on development, internationally binding environmental treaties, and [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") within the WTO system.

## Member of the European Parliament (1999–2004)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Nicholas_Clegg_MEP_portrait.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Clegg_MEP_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Clegg_MEP_portrait.jpg)

Clegg's 1999 portrait as an MEP

In 1998, Clegg was selected as the lead Liberal Democrat candidate for the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands_(European_Parliament_constituency) "East Midlands (European Parliament constituency)") constituency; the following year, [Paddy Ashdown](/wiki/Paddy_Ashdown "Paddy Ashdown") was first to tip him as a politician to watch.[[50]](#cite_note-cleggbackground-53) On his election in 1999, he was the first Liberal parliamentarian elected in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") since [Ernest Pickering](/wiki/Ernest_Pickering_(politician) "Ernest Pickering (politician)") was elected MP for [Leicester West](/wiki/Leicester_West "Leicester West") in 1931, and was credited with helping to significantly boost the Liberal Democrat poll rating in the region in the six months after his election. Clegg worked extensively during his time as an MEP to support the party in the region, not least in [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Chesterfield (UK Parliament constituency)") where [Paul Holmes](/wiki/Paul_Holmes_(Chesterfield_MP) "Paul Holmes (Chesterfield MP)") was elected as MP in [2001](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election"). Clegg helped persuade Conservative MEP [Bill Newton Dunn](/wiki/Bill_Newton_Dunn "Bill Newton Dunn") to defect to the Liberal Democrats, with Newton Dunn subsequently succeeding him as MEP for the East Midlands.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

As an MEP, Clegg co-founded the Campaign for Parliamentary Reform, which led calls for reforms to expenses, transparency and accountability in the European Parliament.[[52]](#cite_note-55) He was made Trade and Industry spokesman for the [European Liberal Democrat and Reform group](/wiki/European_Liberal_Democrat_and_Reform_Party "European Liberal Democrat and Reform Party") (ELDR).[[53]](#cite_note-56) In December 2000, Nick Clegg became the Parliament's Draftsman on a complex new EU telecoms law relating to "[local loop unbundling](/wiki/Local_loop_unbundling "Local loop unbundling")"—opening-up telephone networks across Europe to competition.[[54]](#cite_note-57) Clegg decided to leave Brussels in 2002, arguing in an article in *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* newspaper that the battle to persuade the public of the benefits of Europe was being fought at home, not in Brussels.[[55]](#cite_note-58)

In 2004, Clegg explained to the [Select Committee](/wiki/Select_committee_(United_Kingdom) "Select committee (United Kingdom)") on European Union that the aim of MEPs like himself, who had been active in the debate on the EU's negotiating mandate, was to obtain the right to ratify any major WTO deal entered into by the European Union.[[56]](#cite_note-59) That same year he chaired a policy working group for the Liberal Democrats on the [Third Age](/wiki/Old_age#Third_age "Old age"), which focused on the importance of ending the cliff-edge of retirement and providing greater opportunities for older people to remain active beyond retirement. The group developed initial proposals on transforming post offices to help them survive as community hubs, in particular for older people. He served on [Charles Kennedy](/wiki/Charles_Kennedy "Charles Kennedy")'s policy review, "Meeting the Challenge", and the "It's About Freedom" working parties.

Clegg, for four years whilst an MEP, wrote a fortnightly column for *[Guardian Unlimited](/wiki/Guardian_Unlimited "Guardian Unlimited")*. One particular article in 2002 accused [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") of encouraging "condescension" towards Germany. In an article, Clegg wrote that "all nations have a cross to bear, and none more so than Germany with its memories of Nazism. But the British cross is more insidious still. A misplaced sense of superiority, sustained by delusions of grandeur and a tenacious obsession with the last war, is much harder to shake off".[[57]](#cite_note-60) The article was dusted down during the 2010 general election campaign when the *[Daily Mail](/wiki/Daily_Mail "Daily Mail")* interpreted the article as being a "Nazi slur on Britain" and Clegg had begun to feel the full heat of the British tabloid press following his success during the first [leaders' debate](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election_debates "2010 United Kingdom general election debates").[[58]](#cite_note-61)

## Parliamentary candidate

On leaving the European Parliament, Clegg joined political lobbying firm GPlus in April 2004 as a fifth partner:[[59]](#cite_note-62)

> It's especially exciting to be joining GPlus at a time when Brussels is moving more and more to the centre of business concerns. With the EU taking in ten more countries and adopting a new Constitution, organisations need more than ever intelligent professional help in engaging with the EU institutions.

Clegg worked on GPlus clients including [The Hertz Corporation](/wiki/The_Hertz_Corporation "The Hertz Corporation") and [British Gas](/wiki/Centrica "Centrica").[[60]](#cite_note-Ch4FCApil2010-63)

In May 2003, [Richard Allan](/wiki/Richard_Allan "Richard Allan"), the then Liberal Democrat MP for [Sheffield Hallam](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Hallam (UK Parliament constituency)"), announced his intention to stand down from the House of Commons. Clegg was selected as the prospective parliamentary candidate. He took up a part-time teaching position in the politics department of the [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield"), combining it with ongoing EU consultancy work with GPlus. He also gave a series of seminar lectures in the [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations") Department of the University of Cambridge.

## Member of Parliament (2005–2017)

Clegg worked closely with Allan throughout the campaign in Sheffield Hallam—including starring in a local pantomime—and won the seat in the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") with over 50% of the vote, and a majority of 8,682.[[61]](#cite_note-64) This result represents one of the smallest swings away from a party in a seat where an existing MP has been succeeded by a newcomer (4.3%). He also served as treasurer and secretary of the [All-Party Parliamentary Group on National Parks](/wiki/All-Party_Parliamentary_Group_on_National_Parks "All-Party Parliamentary Group on National Parks"), a particular interest given that his constituency includes part of the [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District_National_Park "Peak District National Park").[[62]](#cite_note-65)

Following his election to parliament, Clegg was promoted by leader [Charles Kennedy](/wiki/Charles_Kennedy "Charles Kennedy") to be the party's spokesperson on Europe, focusing on the party's preparations for an expected referendum on the [European constitution](/wiki/European_constitution "European constitution") and acting as deputy to Foreign Affairs Spokesperson [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell"). Clegg's ability to articulate liberal values at a very practical level quickly lent him prominence, with many[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] already seeing him as a future Liberal Democrat leader. Following the resignation of Kennedy on 7 January 2006, Clegg was touted as a possible leadership contender.[[63]](#cite_note-66) He was quick to rule himself out however instead declaring his support for [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell") ahead of his former colleague in the European Parliament [Chris Huhne](/wiki/Chris_Huhne "Chris Huhne"),[[64]](#cite_note-67) with Campbell going on to win the [ballot](/wiki/2006_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2006 Liberal Democrats leadership election"). Clegg had been a signatory to the letter circulated by [Vince Cable](/wiki/Vince_Cable "Vince Cable") prior to Kennedy's resignation, which stated his opposition to working under Kennedy's continued leadership.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

### Liberal Democrats' Home Affairs spokesperson

Following the 2006 leadership election, Clegg was promoted to be [Home Affairs spokesperson](/wiki/Liberal_Democrat_Home_Affairs_spokesperson "Liberal Democrat Home Affairs spokesperson"), replacing [Mark Oaten](/wiki/Mark_Oaten "Mark Oaten"). In this job he spearheaded the Liberal Democrats' defence of civil liberties, proposing a Freedom Bill to repeal what he described as "unnecessary and illiberal legislation",[[66]](#cite_note-69) campaigning against [Identity Cards](/wiki/National_identity_card_(United_Kingdom) "National identity card (United Kingdom)") and the retention of innocent people's DNA, and arguing against excessive counter-terrorism legislation. He has campaigned for prison reform, a liberal approach to immigration, and defended the [Human Rights Act](/wiki/Human_Rights_Act_1998 "Human Rights Act 1998") against ongoing attacks from across the political spectrum. In January 2007, Clegg launched the 'We Can Cut Crime!' campaign, proposing real action at a national level and acting to cut crime where the Liberal Democrats are in power locally.[[67]](#cite_note-wecancutcrime-70)

### Sir Menzies Campbell's resignation

Clegg caused a degree of controversy when, at the Liberal Democrat party conference in 2007, he admitted his leadership ambitions to journalists at a fringe event.[[68]](#cite_note-Clegg_admits_leadership_ambitions-71) The admission followed a period of increased media speculation about Sir [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell")'s leadership, which the admission by Clegg did nothing to reduce and resulted in a rebuke by some of his frontbench colleagues.[[68]](#cite_note-Clegg_admits_leadership_ambitions-71) This followed a report from [Kevin Maguire](/wiki/Kevin_Maguire_(journalist) "Kevin Maguire (journalist)") in the *[New Statesman](/wiki/New_Statesman "New Statesman")* that Clegg had failed to hide his disloyalty to Campbell's leadership.[[69]](#cite_note-72) Campbell eventually resigned on 15 October 2007, saying that questions about his leadership were "getting in the way of further progress by the party".[[70]](#cite_note-73)

## Leader of the Liberal Democrats (2007–2015)

### Election to the leadership

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Vince_Cable_and_Nick_Clegg_by_the_budget_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Vince_Cable_and_Nick_Clegg_by_the_budget_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vince_Cable_and_Nick_Clegg_by_the_budget_(cropped).jpg)

Clegg by the [2009 budget](/wiki/2009_United_Kingdom_budget "2009 United Kingdom budget")

After Campbell's resignation, Clegg was regarded by much of the media as front-runner in the [leadership election](/wiki/2007_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2007 Liberal Democrats leadership election").[[71]](#cite_note-74)[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76) The BBC's Political Editor [Nick Robinson](/wiki/Nick_Robinson_(journalist) "Nick Robinson (journalist)") stated the election would be a two-horse race between Clegg and [Chris Huhne](/wiki/Chris_Huhne "Chris Huhne") who had stood against Campbell in the 2006 election.[[74]](#cite_note-77) On Friday 19 October 2007, Clegg launched his bid to become leader of the Liberal Democrats.[[75]](#cite_note-78) Clegg and Huhne clashed in the campaign over [Trident](/wiki/UK_Trident_programme "UK Trident programme") but were largely in agreement on many other issues. It was announced on 18 December that he had won.[[76]](#cite_note-79) Clegg was appointed to the [Privy Council](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Most_Honourable_Privy_Council "Her Majesty's Most Honourable Privy Council") (PC) on 30 January 2008, and affirmed his membership on 12 March 2008.

In his acceptance speech upon winning the leadership contest, Clegg declared himself to be "a liberal by temperament, by instinct and by upbringing" and that he believes "Britain [is] a place of tolerance and [pluralism](/wiki/Cultural_pluralism "Cultural pluralism")". He has stated that he feels "a profound antagonism for prejudice of all sorts".[[29]](#cite_note-ekklesia-32) He declared his priorities as: defending civil liberties; devolving the running of public services to parents, pupils and patients; and protecting the environment.[[77]](#cite_note-80)

In an interview on [BBC Radio 5 Live](/wiki/BBC_Radio_5_Live "BBC Radio 5 Live") on the morning after his election to the leadership, Clegg stated that he does not believe in God, but that he has "an immense amount of respect for [people of faith](/wiki/People_of_faith "People of faith")".[[78]](#cite_note-BBC-Clegg-God-81)[[79]](#cite_note-82)[[80]](#cite_note-transcript-83) In 2010, Clegg elaborated on this question, stating: "I was asked a question once in one of those questions where you're only allowed to answer 'yes' or 'no', and I was asked 'Do you believe in God?' As it happens I don't know whether God exists. I'm much more of an agnostic."[[80]](#cite_note-transcript-83)

Clegg resigned as the leader of the Liberal Democrats after the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"). He said the results were "immeasurably more crushing and unkind than he feared".[[81]](#cite_note-84) He was succeeded by [Tim Farron](/wiki/Tim_Farron "Tim Farron") after a [leadership election](/wiki/2015_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2015 Liberal Democrats leadership election").

#### *GQ* magazine interview controversy

In March 2008, *[GQ](/wiki/GQ "GQ")* magazine ran with an interview conducted by [Piers Morgan](/wiki/Piers_Morgan "Piers Morgan") in which Clegg admitted to sleeping with "no more than 30" women.[[82]](#cite_note-85) Senior Lib Dem MPs defended his comments; [Lembit Öpik](/wiki/Lembit_%C3%96pik "Lembit Öpik") said it showed "you can be a human being and a party Leader", and [Norman Lamb](/wiki/Norman_Lamb "Norman Lamb") that "Nick tries to be absolutely straight in everything that he does, and that might sometimes get him into trouble but he will build a reputation for being honest and straightforward."[[83]](#cite_note-86) Speaking to the BBC about the interview Clegg said "wisdom with hindsight is an easy thing" as what had been a split second response had been "taken out of context, interpreted, over interpreted and so on".[[84]](#cite_note-87)

### Relationships with the frontbench

Upon his election Clegg appointed leadership rival Huhne as his replacement as Home Affairs spokesperson and following his strong performances as acting party leader, Vince Cable was retained as the main Treasury spokesperson. Media commentators noted that the Clegg-Huhne-Cable triumvirate provided the Liberal Democrats with an effective political team for the coming years.[[85]](#cite_note-88) On 5 March 2008, Clegg suffered a real test following the resignation of three of his front bench team. [David Heath](/wiki/David_Heath_(politician) "David Heath (politician)"), [Alistair Carmichael](/wiki/Alistair_Carmichael "Alistair Carmichael") and [Tim Farron](/wiki/Tim_Farron "Tim Farron") had been told to abstain in the vote for a referendum on the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Lisbon_Treaty "Lisbon Treaty") but had wanted to vote in favour and so defied the whip. In addition to the three frontbenchers, a further 12 more backbench LibDem MPs also defied the whip and voted "yes". Clegg said "though we have disagreed on this issue I fully understand and respect their strongly held views on the subject.... However, as they have recognised, the shadow cabinet cannot operate effectively unless the principle of collective responsibility is maintained."[[86]](#cite_note-89)

The resignations happened not long after [Michael Martin](/wiki/Michael_Martin,_Baron_Martin_of_Springburn "Michael Martin, Baron Martin of Springburn"), the [Speaker of the House of Commons](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)"), had on 26 February 2008 blocked calls by the Liberal Democrats for an "in or out" referendum on Britain's EU membership. The Speaker's authority was called into question when, led by Nick Clegg, the Liberal Democrats marched out of the House of Commons, calling the Speaker's decision a constitutional "outrage". Just moments earlier, frontbench foreign affairs spokesman for the party [Ed Davey](/wiki/Ed_Davey "Ed Davey") had been expelled from the chamber by the Speaker's deputy, Sir [Michael Lord](/wiki/Michael_Lord "Michael Lord"), for further challenging the ruling.[[87]](#cite_note-90)

In November 2008, Clegg suffered more allegations of difficulties with the front bench following an article in the *[Daily Mirror](/wiki/Daily_Mirror "Daily Mirror")* that reported that Clegg had criticised senior members of his front bench whilst on a plane journey. He told the BBC's Politics Show that "a lot of it is, frankly, fiction".[[88]](#cite_note-91)

> "I believe every single person is extraordinary.
> The tragedy is that we have a society where too many people never get to fulfil that extraordinary potential.
> My view – the liberal view – is that government's job is to help them to do it.
> Not to tell people how to live their lives.
> But to make their choices possible, to release their potential, no matter who they are.
> The way to do that is to take power away from those who hoard it.
> To challenge vested interests.
> To break down privilege.
> To clear out the bottlenecks in our society that block opportunity and block progress.
> And so give everyone a chance to live the life they want."[[89]](#cite_note-92)

Liberal Democrat Manifesto Launch, 14 April 2010

### Attitudes to other parties

In the Commons, Clegg initially concentrated most of his fire on Labour and Prime Minister Gordon Brown, but in the autumn of 2009 began also focusing on [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") and the Conservatives.[[90]](#cite_note-93) Clegg rejected an appeal from Cameron for their two parties to work together.[[91]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-94) Clegg argued that the Conservatives were totally different from his party, and that the Lib Dems were the true "progressives" in UK politics.[[91]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-94) At the 2009 party conference in Bournemouth, he accused the Conservatives of "simply believing it is their turn" and claimed that come the election the "choice before people is the choice between fake, phoney change from Cameron's Conservatives, and real change the Liberal Democrats offer".

### Parliamentary expenses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Clegg_Victory_for_the_Gurkhas.jpg/250px-Clegg_Victory_for_the_Gurkhas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clegg_Victory_for_the_Gurkhas.jpg)

Clegg with [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") and [Chris Huhne](/wiki/Chris_Huhne "Chris Huhne") in 2009

Clegg became the first party leader in modern political history to call for a Speaker of the House of Commons to resign, describing then-Speaker [Michael Martin](/wiki/Michael_Martin,_Baron_Martin_of_Springburn "Michael Martin, Baron Martin of Springburn"), following his handling of the expenses scandal, as a defender of the status quo and an obstacle to the reform of Parliament.[[37]](#cite_note-typepad1-40)[[92]](#cite_note-95)

In response to revelations about MPs' expenses, Clegg set out his plans for reform of Parliament in *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*.[[93]](#cite_note-96) Speaking about the plans, he said: "let us bar the gates of Westminster and stop MPs leaving for their summer holidays until this crisis has been sorted out, and every nook and cranny of our political system has been reformed." He argued for the "reinvention of British politics" within 100 days, calling for a commitment to accept the Kelly expenses report in full; the power to [recall](/wiki/Recall_election "Recall election") members suspended for misconduct; [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") reform; reform of party funding; fixed-term parliaments; enabling legislation for a referendum on [AV+](/wiki/AV%2B "AV+"); and changes to House of Commons procedure to reduce executive power.[[6]](#cite_note-takebackpower1-9)

Shortly ahead of the election, Clegg was asked about his own expenses by [Andrew Neil](/wiki/Andrew_Neil "Andrew Neil") of the BBC. Clegg allegedly claimed the full amount permissible under the Additional Cost Allowance, including claims for food, gardening and redecorating his second home. *The Telegraph* also said Clegg claimed £80 for international call charges, a claim he said he would repay.[[94]](#cite_note-97)

### Perspective

Clegg aimed to modernise the Liberal Democrat Party at the same time as maintaining its traditions of political and philosophical [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"). In 2011, he told a party conference that the Liberal Democrats were [radical centrist](/wiki/Radical_centrist "Radical centrist") in orientation: "Our opponents try to divide us with their outdated labels of left and right. But we are not on the left and we are not on the right. We have our own label: Liberal. We are liberals and we own the freehold to the centre ground of British politics. Our politics is the politics of the radical centre."[[95]](#cite_note-98)

### Policies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Nick_Clegg_being_presented_a_Gurkha_Hat%2C_by_a_Gurkha_veteran.jpg/250px-Nick_Clegg_being_presented_a_Gurkha_Hat%2C_by_a_Gurkha_veteran.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Clegg_being_presented_a_Gurkha_Hat,_by_a_Gurkha_veteran.jpg)

Clegg being presented with a Gurkha hat by a Gurkha veteran during his [Maidstone](/wiki/Maidstone "Maidstone") visit to celebrate the success of their joint campaign for the right to live in Britain, 2009

When he became leader of the Liberal Democrats, Clegg called for more choice for patients on waiting lists in the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") (NHS), giving them the option to go private and to be funded by the NHS if they wish; a substantial tax cut to "put more money back into the pockets of people", better action on the environment, the abandonment of Britain's Trident missile-defence system, [fixed-term parliaments](/wiki/Fixed-term_election "Fixed-term election"); devolving more power to local councils; giving constituents the power to force a by-election if their MP was found responsible for serious wrongdoing; and a slimming of government across the board.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Clegg campaigned to cut spending on defence projects such as [Eurofighter](/wiki/Eurofighter "Eurofighter") as well as the [UK Trident programme](/wiki/UK_Trident_programme "UK Trident programme").[[97]](#cite_note-100) As regards public spending, at the party's 2009 conference in Bournemouth Clegg argued for "savage" spending cuts and said politicians need to treat voters "like grown ups" whilst accusing the Labour and Conservative parties of indulging in "childish games" over the "c-word".[[98]](#cite_note-101)

### Gurkha campaign

On 29 April 2009 the Liberal Democrats proposed in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") to offer all Gurkhas an equal right of residence; the motion resulted in a defeat for the Government by 267 votes to 246. It was the only first day motion defeat for a government since 1978. On speaking about the result Clegg said "this is an immense victory [...] for the rights of Gurkhas who have been waiting so long for justice, a victory for Parliament, a victory for decency". He added that it was "the kind of thing people want this country to do".[[99]](#cite_note-102)

On 21 May 2009, the Home Secretary [Jacqui Smith](/wiki/Jacqui_Smith "Jacqui Smith") announced that all Gurkha veterans who retired before 1997 with at least four years' service could settle in the UK. The actress and daughter of Gurkha corps Major James Lumley, [Joanna Lumley](/wiki/Joanna_Lumley "Joanna Lumley"), who had highlighted the treatment of the Gurkhas and campaigned for their rights, commented: "This is the welcome we have always longed to give".[[100]](#cite_note-103)

## Deputy Prime Minister (2010–2015)

Main articles: [Nick Clegg's tenure as Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Nick_Clegg%27s_tenure_as_Deputy_Prime_Minister "Nick Clegg's tenure as Deputy Prime Minister") and [Cameron–Clegg coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Prime_Minister%2C_David_Cameron_and_Deputy_Prime_Minister%2C_Nick_Clegg._%284751661312%29.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister%2C_David_Cameron_and_Deputy_Prime_Minister%2C_Nick_Clegg._%284751661312%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister,_David_Cameron_and_Deputy_Prime_Minister,_Nick_Clegg._(4751661312).jpg)

Clegg with Cameron in 2010

The morning after the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election") presented the country with no single political party able to form a government that would command a majority in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). In light of this reality, Cameron went public and gave a "big, open and comprehensive offer" to the Liberal Democrats' leader and said that he wanted to open up negotiations with them to form Britain's first [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") since [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s [war ministry](/wiki/Churchill_war_ministry "Churchill war ministry") during the Second World War. In reply, Clegg said that he had always maintained that the party with the most seats and the most votes should have the right to seek to govern.[[101]](#cite_note-104)

Following the announcement, teams of negotiators from both parties formulated what would become the [Coalition Agreement](/wiki/Conservative%E2%80%93Liberal_Democrat_coalition_agreement "Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition agreement") which would form the basis of their partnership together.[[102]](#cite_note-105) [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown")'s resignation on 11 May 2010 meant that Cameron was invited by the Queen to form a government[[103]](#cite_note-106) and a coalition with the Liberal Democrats was agreed, with Clegg as the [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and [Lord President of the Council](/wiki/Lord_President_of_the_Council "Lord President of the Council").[[104]](#cite_note-107) He was also made Minister for Constitutional and Political Reform, which was a key point for the Liberal Democrats during the creation of the coalition. Of the 57 Liberal Democrat MPs, only two ([Charles Kennedy](/wiki/Charles_Kennedy "Charles Kennedy") and [John Leech](/wiki/John_Leech_(politician) "John Leech (politician)")) refused to support the Conservative Coalition agreement.[[105]](#cite_note-nextleft.org-108) Clegg, as Deputy Prime Minister, took [Prime Minister's Questions](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Questions "Prime Minister's Questions") (PMQs) when Cameron was unavailable.[[106]](#cite_note-qanda2-109)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Rutte-Clegg.jpg/250px-Rutte-Clegg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rutte-Clegg.jpg)

Clegg with Netherlands Prime Minister [Mark Rutte](/wiki/Mark_Rutte "Mark Rutte") on 15 November 2010

On 5 July 2010, Clegg unveiled plans to have fewer MPs and to hold a referendum on the voting system so that the next general election would be contested under the Alternative Vote system. The [Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Bill](/wiki/Parliamentary_Voting_System_and_Constituencies_Bill "Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Bill") if successful would see the date of the referendum set for 5 May 2011.[[107]](#cite_note-110)[[108]](#cite_note-bbc-111) The bill also introduced plans to reduce the number of MP's in the House of Commons from 650 to 600, something the Labour party attacked as [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering"), as to do this there would need to be boundary changes.[[108]](#cite_note-bbc-111) The referendum on AV was to ask voters if they wish to "adopt the '[alternative vote](/wiki/Alternative_vote "Alternative vote")' system instead of the current 'first past the post' system" for electing MPs". The question required a yes or no answer.[[109]](#cite_note-112) The result of the [referendum](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_Alternative_Vote_referendum "2011 United Kingdom Alternative Vote referendum") was that the alternative vote proposal was defeated by a margin of 2:1.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Nick_Clegg_%282011%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Nick_Clegg_%282011%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Clegg_(2011)_(cropped).jpg)

Clegg in 2011

Clegg also confirmed that the government planned to introduce legislation for five-year fixed-term parliaments, with elections to be held on the first Thursday in May of the fifth year after the previous general election, starting with 7 May 2015. The corresponding bill was presented to parliament on 22 July 2010 and the [Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011](/wiki/Fixed-term_Parliaments_Act_2011 "Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011") received Royal Assent on 15 September 2011.

On 21 July 2010, Clegg became the first Liberal Democrat leader to answer for [Prime minister's questions](/wiki/Prime_minister%27s_questions "Prime minister's questions").[[110]](#cite_note-113) He courted controversy during the exchange when at the despatch box he attacked Shadow Secretary of State for Justice and Shadow Lord Chancellor [Jack Straw](/wiki/Jack_Straw "Jack Straw") for the decision to invade Iraq, saying "perhaps one day you could account for your role in the most disastrous decision of all, which is the illegal [invasion of Iraq](/wiki/Invasion_of_Iraq "Invasion of Iraq")." Despite having long-held views about the issue, the comment was controversial, as it did not reflect the policy of the government, which was that the legality of the war in Iraq was currently being studied by the Iraq inquiry.[[111]](#cite_note-114)

The issue of student financing had been considered one of the flagship policies of the Liberal Democrats with all of the party's MPs, including Clegg, signing the [Vote for Students pledge](/wiki/Vote_for_Students_pledge "Vote for Students pledge") to oppose any increase in student tuition fees prior to the 2010 general election.[[112]](#cite_note-115) As part of the coalition agreement the Lib Dems abandoned their pledge to oppose any increase in tuition fees but gained permission to abstain on any vote relating to the increase of tuition fees.[[113]](#cite_note-116) Clegg wrote to his MPs saying that he had "struggled endlessly" with the issue and said that departing from the pledge he had made prior to the election would be "one of the most difficult decisions of my political career". Defending recommendations of the [Browne Review](/wiki/Browne_Review "Browne Review"), Clegg said that poorer students would pay less since the income level at which students needed to earn before beginning to pay off their student loan would rise from £15,000 to £21,000.[[114]](#cite_note-117) On 19 September 2012, Clegg apologised, not for breaking his pledge, but for having "made a promise we weren't absolutely sure we could deliver".[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119) The apology was [parodied in a song](/wiki/Nick_Clegg_Says_I%27m_Sorry_(The_Autotune_Remix) "Nick Clegg Says I'm Sorry (The Autotune Remix)").[[117]](#cite_note-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Nick_Clegg_election_infobox_%282015%29.jpg/250px-Nick_Clegg_election_infobox_%282015%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Clegg_election_infobox_(2015).jpg)

Clegg in 2014

On 14 October 2010, Clegg delivered a speech at a school in [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield,_Derbyshire "Chesterfield, Derbyshire"), at which he announced the government's intention to spend £7 billion on a 'fairness premium' designed to see extra support going to the poorest pupils over the course of the parliament.[[118]](#cite_note-121) The package announced would provide 15 hours a week free nursery education for the poorest two-year-olds and a 'pupil premium' which would be given to schools to help those pupils eligible for free school meals worth £2.5 billion a year.[[119]](#cite_note-122) On 20 October 2010, the plans for the 'fairness premium' were introduced by the Treasury as part of the spending review which said that the money would be introduced over the period of the review which "will support the poorest in the early years and at every stage of their education".[[120]](#cite_note-123)

In June 2011, Clegg proposed that more than 46 million people would be handed shares in Royal Bank of Scotland and Lloyds Banking Group under the "people's bank" plan. The plan proposes that ordinary voters would be able to profit from any increase in the value of their shares once the Treasury has recouped taxpayers' money used for the bail-out – an offer that could eventually be worth up to £1,000 to householders. Clegg said that it was "psychologically immensely important" for people to be given a stake in the banks in the wake of the financial crisis.[[121]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/PM-DPM-St_David%27s_Day_Agreement_announcement.jpg/250px-PM-DPM-St_David%27s_Day_Agreement_announcement.jpg)](/wiki/File:PM-DPM-St_David%27s_Day_Agreement_announcement.jpg)

Clegg with Cameron in 2015

In August 2012, after [reform of the House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Reform_Bill_2012 "House of Lords Reform Bill 2012") was abandoned, Clegg said the Conservatives had defied the Coalition agreement by trying to "pick and choose" which items of Government policy they support. Clegg also revealed the Conservatives rejected his suggestion of a "last ditch" compromise to save both policies.[[122]](#cite_note-125) In September 2012, Clegg formally announced that he was "regrettably" withdrawing proposals to reform the Lords in the face of overwhelming opposition from Conservative MPs. He signalled he would exact his revenge by refusing to sack any Liberal Democrat minister who voted against changes to MPs' boundaries – which is Government policy – in retaliation over the Lords reform débâcle. Traditionally party leaders are offered peerages when they leave the House of Commons. When asked in the House of Commons if he would take a seat in the Lords, he said: "No", adding: "I personally will not take a seat in an unreformed House of Lords. It just sticks in the throat."[[123]](#cite_note-126)

## Opposition (2015–2017)

Clegg retained his Sheffield Hallam seat in the 2015 election, despite a strong campaign from Labour which achieved a 17% swing in its favour. Clegg supported the [Britain Stronger in Europe](/wiki/Britain_Stronger_in_Europe "Britain Stronger in Europe") campaign in the [2016 European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"), as did all other Liberal Democrat MPs, and became the Lib Dem spokesperson for [Exiting the European Union](/wiki/Department_for_Exiting_the_European_Union "Department for Exiting the European Union") and [International Trade](/wiki/Department_for_International_Trade "Department for International Trade"). In the 2017 general election, Clegg was unseated by [Jared O'Mara](/wiki/Jared_O%27Mara "Jared O'Mara") of the Labour Party.

## Career after Parliament

### Books

Clegg released a memoir and political manifesto, *[Politics: Between the Extremes](/w/index.php?title=Politics:_Between_the_Extremes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Politics: Between the Extremes (page does not exist)")* in September 2016. This was followed in October 2017 by *[How to Stop Brexit (And Make Britain Great Again)](/wiki/How_to_Stop_Brexit_(And_Make_Britain_Great_Again) "How to Stop Brexit (And Make Britain Great Again)")*, which made the case that [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") was not inevitable. His third book, *[How to Save the Internet: The Threat to Global Connection in the Age of AI and Political Conflict](/wiki/How_to_Save_the_Internet:_The_Threat_to_Global_Connection_in_the_Age_of_AI_and_Political_Conflict "How to Save the Internet: The Threat to Global Connection in the Age of AI and Political Conflict")* was released in September 2025.

### Brexit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Nick_Clegg%2C_Europe_Spokesperson%2C_Liberal_Democrats_%282016-17%29%3B_Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom_%282010-15%29%3B_Leader%2C_Liberal_Democrats_%282007-15%29_%2835834379946%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Clegg,_Europe_Spokesperson,_Liberal_Democrats_(2016-17);_Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom_(2010-15);_Leader,_Liberal_Democrats_(2007-15)_(35834379946).jpg)

Clegg giving a speech in 2017

In May 2018, he joined [David Miliband](/wiki/David_Miliband "David Miliband") and [Nicky Morgan](/wiki/Nicky_Morgan "Nicky Morgan") calling for a [soft Brexit](/wiki/Soft_Brexit "Soft Brexit").[[124]](#cite_note-127) The next month he appeared at a [People's Vote](/wiki/People%27s_Vote "People's Vote") march in London to mark the second anniversary of the [EU referendum of 2016](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum"). People's Vote was a campaign group calling for a public vote on the final Brexit deal between Britain and the [EU](/wiki/EU "EU").[[125]](#cite_note-128)

In July 2019, Clegg said that "aggressive and regressive [English nationalism](/wiki/English_nationalism "English nationalism")" had taken over the Conservative Party in their competition with [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), the leader of the Brexit Party (later rebranded [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK")) established in November 2018. When asked how he expected the next few years to unfold, Clegg told the *[New Statesman](/wiki/New_Statesman "New Statesman")* that "the clock is now ticking for the end of the union of the United Kingdom".[[126]](#cite_note-129)

### Broadcasting and media

From January 2013 until 2015, Clegg presented a weekly radio show on [LBC](/wiki/LBC "LBC") called *Call Clegg*.[[127]](#cite_note-130)[[128]](#cite_note-131) Initially broadcast in the London area, the programme went national along with LBC in February 2014.[[129]](#cite_note-132) The programme was nominated for two [Radio Academy Awards](/wiki/Radio_Academy_Awards "Radio Academy Awards") in 2014.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

Between April and October 2018, Clegg fronted a podcast called *Anger Management with Nick Clegg*,[[131]](#cite_note-134) in which he interviewed known persons about the politics of anger. Since the first episode, it has been featured in *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* under Podcast of the Week.[[132]](#cite_note-135)

A party political broadcast in which Clegg apologised for the Liberal Democrats breaking the promise over tuition fees was remixed into a song, "[Nick Clegg Says I'm Sorry](/wiki/Nick_Clegg_Says_I%27m_Sorry "Nick Clegg Says I'm Sorry")" by [The Poke](/wiki/The_Poke "The Poke") and Alex Ross, and sold on iTunes as a charity single. The song charted on 23 September 2012 at number 143 in the [Official UK Singles Charts](/wiki/Official_UK_Singles_Charts "Official UK Singles Charts") before climbing to 104 the following week.[[133]](#cite_note-136) In his 2010 production Dandelion Mind, comedian [Bill Bailey](/wiki/Bill_Bailey "Bill Bailey") sang "Nick Clegg you don't have to wear that dress tonight, walk the streets for money, you don't have to sell your body to the right" to the tune of "[Roxanne](/wiki/Roxanne_(The_Police_song) "Roxanne (The Police song)")".[[134]](#cite_note-137)

### Facebook

In June 2018, vice-president of global affairs and communications at [Facebook, Inc.](/wiki/Facebook,_Inc. "Facebook, Inc.") [Elliot Schrage](/wiki/Elliot_Schrage "Elliot Schrage") resigned his position.[[135]](#cite_note-Harris_Oct_2021-138) After talks with the Facebook leadership and [Richard Allan, Baron Allan of Hallam](/wiki/Richard_Allan,_Baron_Allan_of_Hallam "Richard Allan, Baron Allan of Hallam"), Facebook's Director of Policy in Europe and the Liberal Democrat MP for Sheffield Hallam before Clegg, in October 2018 Clegg was hired as a lobbyist and public relations officer, replacing Schrage as vice-president, Global Affairs and Communications.[[136]](#cite_note-auto-139)[[137]](#cite_note-auto1-140)[[138]](#cite_note-141) He joined Facebook because he was "convinced that the culture is changing" and that "lawmakers need to have a serious conversation about whether data-intensive companies allow other companies to share and use data". He admitted that the [Cambridge Analytica data scandal](/wiki/Cambridge_Analytica_data_scandal "Cambridge Analytica data scandal") had "rocked Facebook to its very foundations" and told the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") that the company "hadn't done enough in the past" in regards to data privacy.[[139]](#cite_note-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/UK_Government_hosts_AI_Summit_at_Bletchley_Park_%2853305529255%29.jpg/250px-UK_Government_hosts_AI_Summit_at_Bletchley_Park_%2853305529255%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UK_Government_hosts_AI_Summit_at_Bletchley_Park_(53305529255).jpg)

Clegg at the [AI Safety Summit](/wiki/AI_Safety_Summit_2023 "AI Safety Summit 2023") in 2023

In April 2019, Clegg was accused by [Věra Jourová](/wiki/V%C4%9Bra_Jourov%C3%A1 "Věra Jourová"), [European Commissioner](/wiki/European_Commissioner "European Commissioner") for Justice, Consumers and Gender Equality, in a letter signed by a number of senior EU civil servants, of misunderstanding EU law, stating that proposed new Facebook guidelines on political advertising would "hinder the exercise of EU electoral rights."[[140]](#cite_note-143) In May 2019, he rejected calls by American presidential candidates [Elizabeth Warren](/wiki/Elizabeth_Warren "Elizabeth Warren") and [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"), as well as Facebook co-founder [Chris Hughes](/wiki/Chris_Hughes "Chris Hughes") to [break Facebook up](/wiki/Competition_law "Competition law"), saying that Facebook was "a great American success story" and that "I don't think it's a very American tradition to start penalizing success."[[141]](#cite_note-144) In June 2019, Clegg said there was "absolutely no evidence" that Russia had influenced the outcome of the EU referendum using Facebook. He said that the company was working towards greater regulation of technology firms.[[142]](#cite_note-145) In October 2019, [Damian Collins](/wiki/Damian_Collins "Damian Collins"), chair of the British House of Commons [Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee](/wiki/Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport_Committee "Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee"), formally asked Clegg to explain why Facebook had exempted political statements from fact-checking guidelines.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

In 2020, Clegg helped lead the creation of the [Facebook Oversight Board](/wiki/Facebook_Oversight_Board "Facebook Oversight Board"), which reported to him, convincing former Danish Prime Minister and former [College of Europe](/wiki/College_of_Europe "College of Europe") friend [Helle Thorning-Schmidt](/wiki/Helle_Thorning-Schmidt "Helle Thorning-Schmidt") to serve as its co-chair.[[144]](#cite_note-Politico-Clegg-147) He then played a role in Facebook's [deplatforming of U.S. President Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump_on_social_media#Facebook "Donald Trump on social media") in mid-2021.[[145]](#cite_note-148)

Ahead of the release of the whistleblown [Facebook Files](/wiki/Facebook_Files "Facebook Files") in October 2021, Clegg authored an internal memo saying that there was no evidence Facebook was a main cause in polarisation and that the company does not "profit from polarization, in fact, just the opposite."[[146]](#cite_note-149) He later posted a public statement saying that the reporting on the Files "conferred egregiously false motives to Facebook's leadership and employees," saying that it was "just plain false" that Facebook ignored its own internal research.[[147]](#cite_note-150) After the release of the Files, he then made a number of public interviews defending the company. In one of the interviews, he stated that "I can't give you a yes or no answer" when asked if Facebook played a role in amplifying extremist content ahead of the [2021 United States Capitol attack](/wiki/2021_United_States_Capitol_attack "2021 United States Capitol attack").[[148]](#cite_note-151) Clegg faced criticism for his role in defending the company, with the *[Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* journalist [John Harris](/wiki/John_Harris_(critic) "John Harris (critic)") dubbing him "the [fall guy](/wiki/Fall_guy "Fall guy") for Facebook's failures".[[135]](#cite_note-Harris_Oct_2021-138)[[149]](#cite_note-152)[[150]](#cite_note-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154)[[152]](#cite_note-155) In February 2022, Clegg was promoted to president of global affairs.[[153]](#cite_note-156)

In January 2025, Clegg announced he would step down from his role as president of global affairs and leave Meta within "the next few months". He is set to be succeeded by his current deputy [Joel Kaplan](/wiki/Joel_Kaplan "Joel Kaplan"), a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") who previously served as [White House Deputy Chief of Staff](/wiki/White_House_Deputy_Chief_of_Staff "White House Deputy Chief of Staff") under [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"). The leadership change has been described as an attempt to improve Meta's relations with [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who is set to begin his [second term as U.S. president](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump") 17 days after the announcement.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-15)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-16) At the time of the announcement, *The Guardian* reported that Clegg had sold some $18.4 million (£14.8 million) of Meta shares, while retaining a holding worth $21m.[[154]](#cite_note-157)

## Public image

### Electoral performance and standing in the polls

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Nick_Clegg_official_portrait.jpg/250px-Nick_Clegg_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Clegg_official_portrait.jpg)

Clegg's portrait as Deputy Prime Minister, 2010

After Clegg became leader, the polls were mixed; the Liberal Democrats occasionally polled above 20 points,[[155]](#cite_note-158) averaging around 19%.[[156]](#cite_note-159) In May 2009, the party overtook Labour in an opinion poll (25%–22%) for the first time since the days of its predecessor, the [SDP–Liberal Alliance](/wiki/SDP%E2%80%93Liberal_Alliance "SDP–Liberal Alliance"), in 1987.[[157]](#cite_note-160) Clegg thus became the first Liberal Democrat leader to out-poll Labour in an opinion poll. After Clegg's performance in the first of three [general election debates](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election_debates "2010 United Kingdom general election debates") on 15 April 2010, there was an unprecedented surge of media attention and support for the Liberal Democrats in opinion polls. [ComRes](/wiki/ComRes "ComRes") reported the Liberal Democrats at 24% on the day,[[158]](#cite_note-161) and on 20 April in a [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") poll, the Liberal Democrats were on 34%, one point above the Conservatives, with Labour in third place on 28%.[[159]](#cite_note-162) This success was described as ["Cleggmania"](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Cleggmania "wikt:Cleggmania") by journalists.[[160]](#cite_note-163)

Following the formation of the coalition, support for the Liberal Democrats fell.[[8]](#cite_note-thisislondon1-11) On 8 December 2010, the eve of a House of Commons vote on changes in the [funding of higher education](/wiki/Tuition_fees_(UK) "Tuition fees (UK)"), an opinion poll conducted by [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") recorded voting intention figures of Conservatives 41%, Labour 41%, [other parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of political parties in the United Kingdom") 11% and Liberal Democrats 8%,[[161]](#cite_note-164) the lowest level of support recorded for the Liberal Democrats in any opinion poll since September 1990.[[162]](#cite_note-165)

### Parliamentary by-elections (2008–2010)

Five parliamentary by-elections were held during Clegg's leadership prior to the 2010 general election. At [Crewe and Nantwich](/wiki/Crewe_and_Nantwich "Crewe and Nantwich") the party's share of the vote decreased by 4%. In the subsequent [Henley by-election](/wiki/2008_Henley_by-election "2008 Henley by-election") the party achieved a 1.8% increase in their vote. At the [Norwich North by-election](/wiki/2009_Norwich_North_by-election "2009 Norwich North by-election") the party came third with a 2.2% fall in their vote share. The two Scottish by-elections, [Glenrothes](/wiki/2008_Glenrothes_by-election "2008 Glenrothes by-election") and [Glasgow East](/wiki/2008_Glasgow_East_by-election "2008 Glasgow East by-election"), saw decreases in the Liberal Democrat vote, 8% and 10% respectively.

### 2008 and 2009 local elections

The local election results for the Liberal Democrats during the same period were mixed. In the [2008 local elections](/wiki/2008_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2008 United Kingdom local elections") the Liberal Democrats took second place with 25% of the vote making a net gain of 34 councillors and took control of [Sheffield City Council](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Council "Sheffield City Council"),[[163]](#cite_note-166) but their share of the vote was down 1%. [The next year](/wiki/2009_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2009 United Kingdom local elections") the Liberal Democrats gained [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") but lost both [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset") and [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon") producing a net loss of councils and a net loss of one councillor.[[164]](#cite_note-167) The party however did increase its share of the vote by 3% to 28% beating the Labour Party into third place. In the [European Parliament elections](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") held on the same day, the Liberal Democrats gained a seat but had a slight decrease in their share of the vote, staying in 4th place compared to the previous European elections, behind the two main parties and [UKIP](/wiki/UKIP "UKIP").[[165]](#cite_note-168)

### 2008 London elections

In the [2008 London Assembly elections](/wiki/2008_London_Assembly_election "2008 London Assembly election") the Liberal Democrats were the only one of the three main parties to see a decrease in their share of the vote, and in the [mayoral election](/wiki/2008_London_mayoral_election "2008 London mayoral election") the Liberal Democrat candidate [Brian Paddick](/wiki/Brian_Paddick "Brian Paddick") came third again with a decreased share of the vote.

### 2010 general election

At the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), the Liberal Democrats won 23% of the vote, an improvement of 1%, however they only won 57 seats, 5 fewer than in 2005. No political party had an overall majority, resulting in the nation's first [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament") since [February 1974](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election").[[166]](#cite_note-169) Talks between Cameron, the Conservative Party leader, and Clegg led to an agreed Conservative/Liberal Democrat coalition, enabling [the Queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") to invite Cameron to form a government.

### Parliamentary by-elections (2010–2015)

From the 2010 general election to the 2015 general election, Clegg's Liberal Democrats contested 13 by-elections in Great Britain.[[167]](#cite_note-170) The party scored their first by-election win of Clegg's leadership at [Eastleigh](/wiki/2013_Eastleigh_by-election "2013 Eastleigh by-election") in 2013, with [Mike Thornton](/wiki/Mike_Thornton_(politician) "Mike Thornton (politician)") holding the seat for the Liberal Democrats, despite a 19% swing away from the party. Clegg described the result as an election in which Liberal Democrats "overcame the odds with a stunning victory."[[168]](#cite_note-171)

Earlier by-elections in the parliament had proven less successful. They failed to win [Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/2011_Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth_by-election "2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election")[[169]](#cite_note-172) in January 2011, after they had successfully petitioned to overturn the general election result. They polled 32% of the vote, a small increase on 2010, but lost out to Labour whose vote was up by 10 percentage points. The Liberal Democrats also came second at [Leicester South](/wiki/2011_Leicester_South_by-election "2011 Leicester South by-election") (which they had held between [2004](/wiki/2004_Leicester_South_by-election "2004 Leicester South by-election") and [2005](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election")) in May 2011 with 23% (down 4% on 2010),[[170]](#cite_note-173) and at [Manchester Central](/wiki/2012_Manchester_Central_by-election "2012 Manchester Central by-election") in November 2012 where they polled 9% (down 17%).[[171]](#cite_note-174)

In the remaining nine contests, Liberal Democrats have finished no higher than third place (and in Rotherham finished in an unprecedented 8th position, with just 451 votes, or 2% of the total).[[172]](#cite_note-175) In every by-election except Oldham East and Saddleworth their vote has fallen, with decreases of over 10% recorded at eight of the contests. In six of the 13 by-elections, the party have lost their deposit after failing to poll 5% of the vote – an unusually high number of such lost deposits for a major party.

### 2011 local, Scottish and Welsh elections

A year following the formation of the Coalition Clegg's Liberal Democrats faced poor results in the local elections. In [Scotland](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election") the party lost all its mainland constituency seats, holding only the [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Shetland (Scottish Parliament constituency)") and [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Orkney (Scottish Parliament constituency)") islands. Their constituency vote share also fell from 16% to just 8%[[173]](#cite_note-176) In the [Welsh elections](/wiki/2011_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "2011 National Assembly for Wales election") the party held just one of its 3 constituency seats, that of Welsh leader [Kirsty Williams](/wiki/Kirsty_Williams "Kirsty Williams"), but gained [a regional seat](/wiki/South_Wales_Central_(National_Assembly_for_Wales_electoral_region) "South Wales Central (National Assembly for Wales electoral region)").[[174]](#cite_note-177) In the [2011 local elections](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2011 United Kingdom local elections"), the Lib Dems lost over 700 councillors, and slumped from 25% to 17% in the share of the local council vote, also losing control of Sheffield City Council with the LibDems dropping to the lowest number of councillors in more than 20 years.[[175]](#cite_note-178)

In the [AV referendum](/wiki/AV_referendum "AV referendum"), the Yes vote, supported by the Liberal Democrats, was defeated by 67.9% to 32.1%. In the face of the election results, Clegg told the BBC that Liberal Democrats must "get up, dust ourselves down and move on".[[176]](#cite_note-179)

### 2012 local and London elections

Local elections were held in May 2012 to 185 local authorities in Great Britain, including all 32 councils in Scotland and 21 out of 22 in Wales.

Results again proved poor for the Liberal Democrats, as they won 431 seats in total, a loss of over 300 on the pre-election position.[[177]](#cite_note-180) They also lost overall control of one council ([Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_City_Council "Cambridge City Council"), though the Liberal Democrats hold 21 out of 42 seats, so they exercise control with the mayor's casting vote[[178]](#cite_note-181)). They retained control of the other six councils they were defending in England. Despite the losses, the Liberal Democrat vote share saw a modest increase compared to 2011.

Elections were also held for the Mayoralties of [Salford](/wiki/City_of_Salford "City of Salford") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_City_Council "Liverpool City Council"). Liberal Democrat candidates polled 5% and 6% respectively, with Labour winning both contests.[[179]](#cite_note-182)

In London, elections were held to the [London Assembly](/wiki/2012_London_Assembly_election "2012 London Assembly election") and [Mayoralty](/wiki/2012_London_mayoral_election "2012 London mayoral election"). The Liberal Democrats again selected [Brian Paddick](/wiki/Brian_Paddick "Brian Paddick") as their Mayoral candidate. He polled just 4% of the vote (down from 10% in [2008](/wiki/2008_London_mayoral_election "2008 London mayoral election")), and finished fourth behind the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales").[[180]](#cite_note-183) In the Assembly, the Liberal Democrats also finished behind the Greens across London, and failed to win any of the individual constituency seats. They polled 7% of the vote on the London-wide list (which elects "top-up" candidates to the assembly under a form of proportional representation), which represented a decline of 5% on the previous contest. This meant that the party lost one seat, and was reduced to just two assembly seats, their smallest representation since the formation of the assembly in 2000.[[181]](#cite_note-184)

In the aftermath of the results, Clegg again faced calls to quit as leader,[[182]](#cite_note-185) with former MP [Lembit Öpik](/wiki/Lembit_%C3%96pik "Lembit Öpik") suggesting that Clegg retain his Cabinet position while relinquishing leadership of the party, saying "My empirical view is that we would have done better with a different leader".

### 2012 Police and Crime Commissioner elections

As part of the Coalition Agreement, directly elected [Police and Crime Commissioners](/wiki/Police_and_Crime_Commissioners "Police and Crime Commissioners") were introduced to replace [Police Authorities](/wiki/Police_authority "Police authority").[[183]](#cite_note-186) [Elections](/wiki/2012_England_and_Wales_police_and_crime_commissioner_elections "2012 England and Wales police and crime commissioner elections")[[184]](#cite_note-187) to the new posts took place in November 2012. Liberal Democrats contested 24 of the 41 police force areas, and failed to win any of the contests (and in fact never progressed to the second round of the two-stage count in any of the elections they fought). Their best performance was in [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria_Constabulary "Cumbria Constabulary"), where they polled 22%, while their worst was [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey_Constabulary "Surrey Constabulary") where the took just 6% of the vote.

Despite not winning any contests under their official party label, one Liberal Democrat, [Winston Roddick](/wiki/Winston_Roddick "Winston Roddick") was elected as Police and Crime Commissioner for [North Wales](/wiki/North_Wales_Police "North Wales Police") having stood as an Independent.[[185]](#cite_note-188) Roddick claimed that he had never hidden his party membership and that his campaign was "financed by himself with no donations or backing from any political party and he was an independent candidate in every sense of the word". His campaign also dismissed as "sour grapes" claims from the Labour Party that "the only way in which the Lib Dems thought they could win the election was by presenting themselves as independent."

### 2015 and 2017 general elections

In the 2015 general election, the Liberal Democrats were reduced from 57 seats to 8. Clegg held his Sheffield Hallam seat with a reduced majority. After his party's result, he resigned the party leadership the day after the election.[[186]](#cite_note-189)[[187]](#cite_note-190)

In the 2017 general election, Clegg lost his constituency to Labour candidate [Jared O'Mara](/wiki/Jared_O%27Mara "Jared O'Mara") by a margin of 2,125 votes (4.2%).[[11]](#cite_note-telegraph2-14)

## Personal life

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Clegg_and_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Dur%C3%A1ntez.jpg/250px-Clegg_and_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Dur%C3%A1ntez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clegg_and_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Dur%C3%A1ntez.jpg)

Clegg with his wife [Miriam](/wiki/Miriam_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Dur%C3%A1ntez "Miriam González Durántez") holding their third son Miguel on 23 February 2009

In September 2000, Clegg married [Miriam González Durántez](/wiki/Miriam_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Dur%C3%A1ntez "Miriam González Durántez"), from [Valladolid](/wiki/Valladolid "Valladolid"), Spain.[[188]](#cite_note-191) They have three sons.[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193) While Clegg has stated that he does not believe in God,[[37]](#cite_note-typepad1-40)[[191]](#cite_note-194) his wife is a Roman Catholic and they are bringing up their children as Catholics. On 16 September 2010, during [Pope Benedict XVI's visit to the United Kingdom](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI%27s_visit_to_the_United_Kingdom "Pope Benedict XVI's visit to the United Kingdom"), Clegg attended the State reception in the grounds of [Holyrood Palace](/wiki/Holyrood_Palace "Holyrood Palace") and was introduced to the Pope by [Her Majesty the Queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II").[[192]](#cite_note-195) Clegg identifies as a feminist.[[193]](#cite_note-196)

Upon being hired by Facebook, Clegg moved to [Atherton, California](/wiki/Atherton,_California "Atherton, California"),[[194]](#cite_note-197) having previously lived in Parkfields, [Putney](/wiki/Putney "Putney"), south west London.[[195]](#cite_note-198) He also has a house in his former constituency close to the [Peak District](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District"), and often walked with his wife near [Stanage Edge](/wiki/Stanage_Edge "Stanage Edge"), which he describes as "one of the most romantic places in the world".[[196]](#cite_note-199) In May 2010 Downing Street announced that Clegg and the [Foreign Secretary](/wiki/Foreign_Secretary_(United_Kingdom) "Foreign Secretary (United Kingdom)") [William Hague](/wiki/William_Hague "William Hague") would share use of [Chevening](/wiki/Chevening "Chevening"), which is typically the official country residence of the Foreign Secretary of the United Kingdom.[[197]](#cite_note-200) In August 2022, Clegg announced he was returning to live in London for personal reasons, including being closer to elderly parents, dividing his working time between London and California.[[198]](#cite_note-telegraph-20220803-201)

When he appeared on *[Desert Island Discs](/wiki/Desert_Island_Discs "Desert Island Discs")* in October 2010, his choice of discs included [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)") and [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead") and his luxury was a "stash of cigarettes".[[199]](#cite_note-DIDiscs_JH-202)[[200]](#cite_note-203) In an interview in April 2011, Clegg stated he dealt with the pressures of political office by reading novels late at night and he "cries regularly to music".[[201]](#cite_note-rosa_prince_clegg-204) He supports [Arsenal F.C.](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")[[202]](#cite_note-205)

"...I have always been very open. I was asked a question once in one of those questions where you're only allowed to answer "yes" or "no", and I was asked "Do you believe in God?" As it happens I don't know whether God exists. I'm much more of an agnostic. But of course when I was asked "yes" or "no", I tried (as I always do) to give a straight answer, and I said "Well, no, I don't know". As it happens I have an immense amount of respect for people of faith. I'm not a man of faith ... [but] my wife is, you know, religious. My children are being brought up my three children are being brought up in her faith. I accompany them to church pretty well every week because, you know, I respect that. Large numbers of my family are, it's just that I am not a man of faith. I know it's fashionable in politics, just as it is fashionable in politics to say that you support a football team even if you haven't since the year of sort of – since you were two years old. It's also fashionable to suddenly pretend that people have faith when they don't. I have always been very open about the fact that I am not a man of faith."
